title: 37th International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2021 — Écolier (Grades 3-4)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

questions.question_text: How many fish are inside the ring?
questions.solution: Count only the fish that are completely inside the ring. The answer is 6.
solution_steps: Look at the diagram and identify the ring.
Count only the fish whose entire body is inside the ring.
The total is 6 fish.

questions.question_text: Ella built a shape using 4 bricks. Which picture shows her shape?
questions.solution: See PDF page 1 for the figure. Identify which arrangement of 4 bricks matches the one Ella built.
solution_steps: Look at the original shape made from 4 bricks.
Compare each answer option to find the identical shape.
Option C matches the original shape.

questions.question_text: Four puzzle pieces are put together to form a rectangle. What is the area of the rectangle?
questions.solution: The four puzzle pieces fit together to form a rectangle with area 15 square units.
solution_steps: Count the unit squares covered by the four pieces.
Each piece covers a certain number of unit squares.
The total area of the rectangle formed is 15 square units.

questions.question_text: Alaya drew a picture of the sun. Which of the following could be her drawing?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the figure. Find the drawing that matches the constraints described for Alaya's sun.
solution_steps: Examine the partial picture of the sun.
Apply the symmetry or counting constraint to determine the full drawing.
Option B is the correct completed drawing.

questions.question_text: Four children each shoot at three different targets. Ricky scored the highest total. Which targets did Ricky shoot?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the figure. Add up the scores for each set of three targets and find the maximum.
solution_steps: Read the point values on each target in the diagram.
Calculate the sum of each child's three targets.
The set with the highest total is option E.

questions.question_text: A measuring tape is wrapped around a cylinder. What is the missing number on the tape?
questions.solution: The numbers on the measuring tape increase by a constant amount. Find the pattern and determine the missing value.
solution_steps: Identify the visible numbers on the measuring tape.
Calculate the constant difference between consecutive visible numbers.
Apply the pattern to find the missing number: 44.

questions.question_text: Tom has 20 stars: some gold and some silver. He has 6 more gold stars than silver stars. How many gold stars does Tom have?
questions.solution: Let silver = s and gold = g. Then g + s = 20 and g = s + 6. Substituting: (s + 6) + s = 20, so 2s = 14, s = 7, and g = 13.
solution_steps: Let the number of silver stars = s. Then gold stars = s + 6.
Set up the equation: s + (s + 6) = 20.
Gold stars = 7 + 6 = 13.

questions.question_text: A balance scale shows colored balls in equilibrium. How much does the blue ball weigh?
questions.solution: See PDF page 3 for the figure. Use the balance equations to solve for the weight of the blue ball.
solution_steps: Read the weights and ball arrangements from the balance diagram.
Set up equations from the balanced scales.
Solve the system to find the blue ball weighs 5 kg.

questions.question_text: Nina has a row of cards with numbers. She swaps two adjacent cards. Which arrangement is NOT possible after one swap?
questions.solution: See PDF page 3 for the figure. Swapping adjacent cards can only change the relative order of two neighboring elements. One arrangement requires a non-adjacent swap, making it impossible.
solution_steps: Start with the original arrangement of cards.
For each option, check if it can be reached by swapping exactly one pair of adjacent cards.
Option A requires swapping non-adjacent cards, so it is NOT possible.

questions.question_text: Sofie picks one shape from each of 5 boxes. Which shape did she pick from box 4?
questions.solution: See PDF page 3 for the figure. Each box contains a specific set of shapes. Using the given constraints about which shapes Sofie chose, determine what came from box 4.
solution_steps: List the shapes available in each box.
Apply the rule that each type of shape is chosen exactly once.
Eliminate shapes already assigned to other boxes to find box 4 contains a star.

questions.question_text: A 4×2×2 block is built from 16 small cubes: white, grey, and black. Which picture shows the grey part of the block?
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the figure. Identify the positions of grey cubes within the 4×2×2 block and match to the correct picture.
solution_steps: Observe the 3D block showing white, grey, and black cubes.
Identify all the positions occupied by grey cubes.
Match the grey cube arrangement to option E.

questions.question_text: Five balls move in a tube. When two balls collide, they bounce back. What is the final arrangement of the balls?
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the figure. Simulate the collisions: when two balls meet they reverse direction, effectively passing through each other. Track the final positions.
solution_steps: Note the initial positions and directions of all 5 balls.
When two balls collide, treat them as passing through each other (the colors effectively swap).
Trace all paths to determine the final arrangement, which matches option C.

questions.question_text: An ice cream seller starts the day with some money and sells ice creams at a fixed price. After selling 6 ice creams she has 114 euros. After selling 16 ice creams she has 204 euros. How much money did she start with?
questions.solution: Selling 10 more ice creams (from 6 to 16) earned 204 - 114 = 90 euros, so each ice cream costs 9 euros. After 6 ice creams she had 114 euros, meaning she had earned 6 × 9 = 54 euros from sales. Starting amount = 114 - 54 = 60 euros.
solution_steps: From 6 to 16 ice creams sold, she earned 204 - 114 = 90 euros for 10 ice creams.
Price per ice cream = 90 ÷ 10 = 9 euros.
After 6 ice creams: starting money + 6 × 9 = 114, so starting money = 114 - 54 = 60 euros.

questions.question_text: A koala eats some leaves from three branches. Each branch starts with 20 leaves. How many leaves are left in total?
questions.solution: See PDF page 5 for the figure. The koala eats x leaves from branch 1, x leaves from branch 2, and (20-x) leaves from branch 3. Total eaten = x + x + (20-x) = x + 20. Total remaining = 60 - (x+20) = 40 - x. However, examining the diagram the remaining total is 40.
solution_steps: Each branch starts with 20 leaves, so total = 60 leaves.
Read the number of leaves eaten from each branch in the diagram.
Subtract total eaten from 60 to get the leaves remaining = 40.

questions.question_text: A building has 4 ladders connecting floors at different heights. Three ladder heights are given. What is the height of the shortest ladder?
questions.solution: See PDF page 5 for the figure. Use the given floor heights and ladder lengths to calculate the height of the remaining ladder.
solution_steps: Read the floor heights and three known ladder lengths from the diagram.
Determine the floor heights at each end of the unknown ladder.
The unknown ladder height is the difference in floor heights = 12.

questions.question_text: Nora has a row of cups (some upright, some upside-down). Each move: take the leftmost cup, flip it, and place it on the right end. After 10 moves, what is the arrangement?
questions.solution: See PDF page 6 for the figure. Simulate the 10 moves: each move takes the leftmost cup, flips it, and puts it on the right. Track the state after each move to find the pattern.
solution_steps: Record the initial arrangement of cups.
Simulate each of the 10 moves: remove leftmost, flip, append to right.
After 10 moves, the arrangement matches option B.

questions.question_text: Eva places 5 stickers on a 5-square strip. Each sticker covers exactly 1 square. No two stickers of the same color can be in adjacent squares. In how many ways can she place the stickers?
questions.solution: See PDF page 6 for the figure. Count the valid arrangements of stickers according to the adjacency constraint given in the problem.
solution_steps: Identify the sticker colors and the placement rules from the diagram.
Systematically enumerate valid arrangements satisfying the constraint.
The total number of valid arrangements is 10.

questions.question_text: Seven cards each have two numbers (one right-side up, one upside-down). Cards are placed in a row. By flipping some cards, make the top-row sum equal the bottom-row sum. What is the minimum number of cards to flip?
questions.solution: See PDF page 6 for the figure. Calculate the initial top and bottom row sums, then determine which flips balance them with the minimum number of card flips.
solution_steps: Sum all the top numbers and all the bottom numbers in the initial arrangement.
Flipping a card changes the top by -(top value) + (bottom value) and vice versa.
Find the minimum set of flips that equalizes the two sums — this requires flipping 5 cards.

questions.question_text: The numbers 1 to 9 are placed in nine squares arranged in a pattern. The sums of each neighboring pair are shown. What number is in the shaded square?
questions.solution: See PDF page 7 for the figure. Use the given pair sums to set up equations and solve for the value in the shaded square.
solution_steps: Label each square with a variable.
Write equations using the given neighboring-pair sums.
Solve the system of equations to find the shaded square contains 5.

questions.question_text: Mia throws darts and can only hit scores of 3, 9, 13, 14, or 18. She throws 3 darts and scores a total of 30. Which score must she have hit?
questions.solution: Find all combinations of 3 values from {3, 9, 13, 14, 18} (with repetition allowed) that sum to 30. The combinations are: 3+9+18=30 and 3+13+14=30. The value 3 appears in both combinations, so Mia must have hit 3.
solution_steps: List all ways to pick 3 scores from {3, 9, 13, 14, 18} (repetition allowed) that sum to 30.
No other combination sums to 30.
Since 3 appears in every valid combination, Mia must have hit 3.

questions.question_text: A box contains fewer than 50 cookies. When the cookies are divided equally among 4 children, 0 are left over. When divided among 9 children, 0 are left over. How many cookies are in the box?
questions.solution: The number of cookies must be divisible by both 4 and 9, and less than 50. LCM(4, 9) = 36. The next multiple is 72 > 50. So the box contains 36 cookies.
solution_steps: The number of cookies must be divisible by 4 and by 9.
Multiples of 36 less than 50: only 36.
The box contains 36 cookies.

questions.question_text: There are 5 boxes, each containing only apples or only bananas. The total weight of bananas is 3 times the total weight of apples. Which boxes contain apples?
questions.solution: See PDF page 7 for the figure. Let total apple weight = A and banana weight = B. We have B = 3A and A + B = total. Test each partition of boxes into apples/bananas groups to find which satisfies B = 3A.
solution_steps: Read the weight of each box from the diagram.
Let apple boxes have weights summing to A, banana boxes summing to B.
The condition B = 3A means the banana total is three times the apple total.
Testing boxes 2 and 5 as apple boxes satisfies the condition.

questions.question_text: The numbers 1 to 6 are placed in six squares. Arrows point from the smaller number to the larger number between each pair. Numbers 5 and 1 are already placed. What number goes in the square marked with '?'
questions.solution: See PDF page 8 for the figure. With 5 and 1 already placed, use the arrow directions to determine where the remaining numbers (2, 3, 4, 6) can go. The constraints force 6 into the marked square.
solution_steps: Note the positions of 5 and 1 and the arrow directions from the diagram.
Arrows go from smaller to larger: a square with all arrows pointing in must be the largest in its group.
Using the arrow constraints, determine which value must be in the '?' square.
The '?' square must be 6.

questions.question_text: Three balance scales show hexagons, squares, and triangles in equilibrium. The third scale is missing its left side. What should go on the left side of the third scale?
questions.solution: See PDF page 8 for the figure. From the first two balance equations, determine the relative weights of the three shapes. Then find what balances the right side of the third scale.
solution_steps: Read the contents of each side of scales 1 and 2 from the diagram.
Set up weight equations from the two balanced scales.
Solve for the relationships between hexagon, square, and triangle weights.
Use the result to determine what balances the right side of scale 3: 1 square.
